narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 40.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. Your profile shows strong alignment with typical applicants in some areas—you're a white male planning full-time studies, and your family's upper-middle income is right around average—but it stands out in academics, where your Year 1 class rank fell in the 2nd decile (much lower than the typical 6th decile), your Year 3 rank was similarly in the 2nd decile (versus the average 6th), your LSAT score of 32 trails the usual 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 is below the standard 3.21. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your Year 3 class rank emerging as the single biggest influence, strongly signaling a higher risk of bar failure because it was so much lower than most students'. Next in impact was your LSAT score, which also pushed the prediction toward failure due to being below par, followed closely by your Year 1 class rank having a similar concerning effect. Your undergraduate GPA, family income, racial background, gender, and full-time status played smaller roles, all nudging slightly toward the same riskier outcome compared to stronger peers. Overall, your academic standings in school deciles, LSAT, and GPA were the dominant drivers, placing you below the typical successful applicant who excels in these areas. While demographics like gender and race matched common profiles without much sway, they couldn't offset the academic gaps. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher than 37, build stronger GPA through grad-level courses if possible, and target top decile rankings in any further studies by honing study habits early. Preparing rigorously for the bar with targeted practice on weak subjects could also demonstrate growth. Many students turn things around by addressing these exact areas—consider tutoring or prep courses tailored to low-ranked performers. You're close in some respects, so persistent improvement could flip the model's view next time. Keep pushing forward; your determination will make the difference.
